topic: A person discovers their mentor has retired without saying goodbye
raw_output:
[story 1]

The office felt cavernous, the usual hum of activity replaced by a dead, echoing silence. I stood by the mahogany desk, the heavy crystal paperweight cool beneath my fingertips, staring at the empty chair across from me. Mr. Silas, my guide, my entire professional landscape, was gone. There was no note, no farewell card tucked into his worn leather briefcase. Just an unscheduled absence, a sudden, stark void where his presence had always anchored my days. My hands began to tremble, the slight vibration making the papers on the desk flutter. I took a shallow breath, trying to steady the rapid pulse hammering against my ribs. Every instinct screamed at me to call, to demand an explanation, but the sheer weight of the silence pressed down on my chest, making the air thick and hard to draw in. I slowly turned, my gaze sweeping the room, searching for any misplaced object, any sign that this was merely a temporary lapse, not a final severing.

[story 2]

I found the notice taped to the door frame—a simple, stark card stating Silas had stepped down. It was tucked beneath a stack of quarterly reports, neatly placed, almost deliberately so. A coldness washed over me, seeping from my toes up through my boots. I picked up the card, the paper surprisingly flimsy in my hand. My throat constricted, making it difficult to form any sound. I paced the length of my workstation, the worn carpet muffling my footsteps, each step feeling monumental. I kept glancing at the closed door, half-expecting him to materialize, perhaps to apologize, to explain the sudden shift in the atmosphere. The quiet of the office amplified the frantic drumming in my ears. I pressed my palms against my eyes for a brief second, needing a moment to recalibrate, before forcing myself to walk toward the exit, the movement stiff and unnatural.

[story 3]

The morning arrived with an oppressive stillness. I arrived at the usual meeting room, the scent of old coffee clinging to the air, but the customary energy was absent. When I walked in, my eyes immediately sought Mr. Silas’s usual seat. It was vacant. A strange, hollow sensation bloomed in my stomach, a sudden plummeting feeling. I circled the room twice, my attention snagging on details: a half-finished crossword puzzle, a book lying open face down. Nothing explained the abrupt vacuum he had left behind. My jaw tightened, a hard knot forming beneath my skin. I stood perfectly still by the window, watching the city move below, feeling utterly unmoored, as if the very ground beneath me had shifted, leaving me suspended in a dizzying uncertainty.
